sections.heading: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions of key terms in telecommunications law
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Telecommunications Act 1999, including 'Authority' as the Info-communications Media Development Authority, 'broadcasting apparatus', 'Chief Executive', 'code of practice', 'common property', 'earthworks', 'equipment', 'Hertzian or radio waves', 'Housing and Development Board', 'installation or plant used for telecommunications', 'message', 'owner', 'premises', 'public telecommunication licensee', 'public telecommunication licensee's installation or plant', 'radio-communication', 'radio-communication service', 'radio-communication system', 'repealed Act', 'securities', 'shares', 'standard of performance', 'street', 'TAS', 'telecommunication cable', 'telecommunication cable detection work', 'telecommunication cable detection worker', 'telecommunication cable detection work licence', 'telecommunication licensee', 'telecommunication line', 'telecommunication service', 'telecommunication system', 'telecommunication system licensee', 'telecommunications', 'Town Council', and other relevant concepts. These definitions establish the legal framework for telecommunications operations in Singapore.

requirements.text: Authority means the Info-communications Media Development Authority established by section 3 of the Info-communications Media Development Authority Act 2016
Broadcasting apparatus and broadcasting service have the meanings given by the Broadcasting Act 1994
Chief Executive means the Chief Executive of the Authority appointed under section 40(2) of the Info-communications Media Development Authority Act 2016
Code of practice means a code of practice issued or approved under section 23 or 30
Common property has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Building (Strata Management) Act 2004
Earthworks includes acts of excavating earth, rock or other material in connection with construction works, laying of cables, soil investigation works, or other construction industry activities
Equipment includes any appliance, apparatus or accessory used or intended for telecommunications
Hertzian or radio waves means electromagnetic waves of frequencies not exceeding 1,000 terahertz propagated in space without any artificial guide
Housing and Development Board means the Housing and Development Board established by section 3 of the Housing and Development Act 1959
Installation or plant used for telecommunications includes all buildings, lands, structures, machinery, equipment, cables, poles and lines used for telecommunications
Message means any sign, signal, writing, image, sound, intelligence or information transmitted by telecommunications

Owner means the person registered as the subsidiary proprietor of a lot under the Land Titles (Strata) Act 1967, or the management corporation having control of common property, or the subsidiary management corporation for limited common property
Premises has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Building Control Act 1989
Public telecommunication licensee means a person designated by the Authority under section 8
Public telecommunication licensee's installation or plant means any installation or plant used for telecommunications belonging to or used by a public telecommunication licensee
Radio-communication means any telecommunication by means of Hertzian or radio waves
Radio-communication service means any service for radio-communications
Radio-communication system means any system used or intended for radio-communications

Repealed Act means the Telecommunication Authority of Singapore Act (Cap. 323, 1.993 Revised Edition) repealed by the Info-communications Development Authority of Singapore Act (Cap. 137A, 2000 Revised Edition)
Securities, in relation to a company, includes shares, debentures, bonds and other securities of the company
Shares includes stock
Standard of performance means a standard of performance issued or approved under section 30
Street includes any way, road, lane, path, passage or open space over which the public have a right of way
TAS means the Telecommunication Authority of Singapore reconstituted by the repealed Act
Telecommunication cable means any cable of a telecommunication system belonging to or under the management or control of a telecommunication system licensee
Telecommunication cable detection work means any work of detecting or locating any underground telecommunication cable
Telecommunication cable detection worker means a person engaged in telecommunication cable detection work
Telecommunication cable detection work licence means a licence for telecommunication cable detection work
Telecommunication licensee means a person licensed to provide telecommunications services
Telecommunication line means a physical connection for telecommunications
Telecommunication service means any service provided through telecommunications
Telecommunication system means any system for providing telecommunications services
Telecommunications means the transmission of information via telecommunications networks
Town Council means the local government body responsible for urban areas

sections.heading: The privilege conferred by section 3 is not infringed by —
sections.title: Exemptions for spectrum rights and licenses
sections.summary: This section outlines exemptions from the privilege conferred by section 3, allowing certain activities related to spectrum rights and telecommunications licenses without infringing on the privilege. It specifies that activities conducted under licenses granted by the Authority do not constitute an infringement of the privilege, and provides for the grant of spectrum rights and licenses with conditions.

requirements.text: The privilege conferred by section 3 is not infringed by activities conducted under licenses granted by the Authority
The Authority may grant spectrum rights unconditionally or subject to conditions, including conditions on sharing and trading of radio frequency spectrum
The Authority may grant spectrum rights to any person or class of persons, or to a particular person
The Authority may specify procedures, requirements, conditions, fees, and charges for the grant of spectrum rights
The Authority may determine the grant of spectrum rights through auction, tender, or exchange for fees
The grant of spectrum rights is subject to payment of fees and charges by the person granted the spectrum right
The fees and charges payable by a person to the Authority are recoverable as a debt due to the Authority
No person may question whether the grant of a spectrum right was effected with the consent of or in accordance with the terms of a general authority given by the Minister

sections.heading: The privilege conferred by section 3 is not infringed by — (cont.)
sections.title: Exemptions for spectrum rights and licenses (continued)
sections.summary: This section continues the exemptions for spectrum rights and licenses, detailing the conditions under which the Authority may grant spectrum rights, the fees payable, and the consequences of non-compliance with the terms of the grant. It specifies that the grant of spectrum rights is at the discretion of the Authority and that the validity of the grant cannot be challenged on the grounds of non-consent from the Minister.

requirements.text: The privilege conferred by section 3 is not infringed by activities conducted under licenses granted by the Authority
The Authority may grant a licence for the use of any satellite orbital slot unconditionally or subject to conditions
The licence may be granted to any person or class of persons, or to a particular person
The licence may include conditions requiring the licensee to enter into agreements with other persons or telecommunication licensees for coordination of radio frequencies
The licence may include conditions requiring the licensee to comply with any direction given by the Authority
The licence may include conditions requiring the licensee to comply with codes of practice and standards of performance
The licence may include conditions requiring the licensee to do or refrain from doing specific things
The grant of a licence is subject to payment of fees for the grant of the licence as prescribed
The licensee must also pay other fees or periodic fees for the duration of the licence
Fees payable under the licence are recoverable as a debt due to the Authority
The licensee is not entitled to refund of any fee paid if the licence is cancelled or suspended, or terminated at the request of the licensee
No person may question whether the grant of a licence was effected with the consent of or in accordance with the terms of a general authority given by the Minister

sections.heading: The Authority may, with the approval of the Minister, designate
sections.title: Authority designates public telecommunication licensees for service operations
sections.summary: The Authority may designate licensed entities to operate telecommunication services in Singapore with Minister approval. Designated entities must perform all functions related to telecommunication systems and services within the Authority's exclusive privilege. This section establishes the mechanism for authorizing which entities can provide telecommunication services in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the authority to designate telecommunication licensees, which impacts cross-border data transfers by determining which entities can legally operate services in Singapore. The designation process requires Minister approval and specifies the scope of operations.

requirements.text: The Authority may designate any person granted a licence under section 5 as a public telecommunication licensee
Designated entities must perform all or any functions relating to telecommunication systems and services in Singapore
Designation requires approval of the Minister

sections.source_quote: 8. The Authority may, with the approval of the Minister, designate any person who has been granted a licence under section 5 as a public telecommunication licensee to perform all or any of the functions relating to the operation and provision of telecommunication systems and services in Singapore within the exclusive privilege of the Authority under this Act.

sections.heading: The Authority may, with the approval of the Minister, designate (cont.)
Subsections (1) and (6) of section 17 do not —
sections.title: Wayleave agreements for telecommunications installations
sections.summary: This section explicitly states that subsections (1) and (6) of section 17 do not affect the right of a public telecommunication licensee to enter into wayleave agreements with landowners for telecommunications installations. It also confirms that existing wayleave agreements in force before December 1, 1999 remain unaffected. The section further states that licensees retain the right to negotiate land or facility use with the State or other persons.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that pre-existing wayleave agreements for telecommunications installations remain valid and unaffected by certain provisions, which may impact cross-border data transfer scenarios involving physical infrastructure.

requirements.text: Subsections (1) and (6) of section 17 do not affect the right of a public telecommunication licensee to enter into wayleave agreements with landowners for telecommunications installations
Subsections (1) and (6) of section 17 do not affect existing wayleave agreements in force immediately before December 1, 1999
Public telecommunication licensees retain the right to negotiate land or facility use with the State or other persons

sections.source_quote: Subsections (1) and (6) of section 17 do not — (a) affect the right of a public telecommunication licensee to enter into an agreement, commonly known as a wayleave agreement, with the owner or occupier of any land for the purpose of laying, placing, carrying or erecting any installation or plant used for telecommunications on the land; (b) affect any such wayleave agreement subsisting immediately before 1 December 1999; or (c) affect the right of a public telecommunication licensee to negotiate the use of land or facilities belonging to the State or any other person.

sections.title: Telecom licensees must comply with dispute resolution procedures for infrastructure disputes
sections.summary: Telecommunication licensees must follow a dispute resolution process when landowners object to infrastructure installation. The Authority mediates disputes, and licensees must take genuine steps to resolve conflicts. If unresolved, licensees must report to the Authority with details of actions taken. The Authority may issue binding directions to resolve disputes, including requiring landowners to allow installation or licensees to install systems. Non-compliance with directions is an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs dispute resolution for telecom infrastructure installations across borders, directly impacting data storage locations and physical infrastructure access for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Telecommunication licensee must give 14 days' written notice to landowner/developer
Landowner may lodge written objection within 14 days
Authority must notify licensee to resolve dispute with objector
Licensee must take genuine steps to resolve dispute with objector
If dispute not resolved, licensee must inform Authority and provide details of steps taken
Authority must notify licensee and objector whether licensee took genuine steps to resolve dispute
If Authority satisfied, must hold inquiry on objection with opportunity to be heard
Authority may direct licensee or landowner to give effect to notice with terms/conditions
Licensee may enter on land/building to do acts after objection period expires
Licensee may enter after objection withdrawn or resolved
Licensee may enter after Authority direction with terms/conditions
Authority may vary, suspend or revoke directions
Failure to comply with direction constitutes offence

sections.source_quote: the licensee must give 14 days' notice to the developer or owner of that land or building, stating as fully and accurately as possible the nature and extent of the acts intended to be done

sections.heading: [30/2016] (cont.)
sections.title: Telecom licensees must resolve disputes with landowners over infrastructure installation
sections.summary: Telecommunication licensees must provide 14-day written notice to landowners before installing infrastructure. Landowners may object within 14 days. The Authority must mediate disputes if objections are made. Licensees must take genuine steps to resolve disputes. If unresolved, licensees must inform the Authority and provide details of steps taken. The Authority may then hold an inquiry and issue directions to resolve the dispute, including requiring landowners to allow installation or licensees to install systems. Failure to comply with directions constitutes an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a dispute resolution mechanism for telecom infrastructure installation across borders, requiring landowners to object within 14 days and mandating mediation by the Authority. It impacts cross-border data transfers by regulating physical infrastructure access and potential data storage locations.

requirements.text: Telecommunication licensee must give 14 days' written notice to landowner/developer stating nature and extent of acts
Landowner may lodge written objection within 14 days of notice
Authority must notify licensee to resolve dispute with objector
Licensee must take genuine steps to resolve dispute with objector
If dispute not resolved, licensee must inform Authority and provide details of steps taken
Authority must notify licensee and objector whether licensee took genuine steps to resolve dispute
If Authority satisfied, must hold inquiry on objection with opportunity to be heard
Authority may direct licensee or landowner to give effect to notice with terms/conditions
Licensee may enter on land/building to do acts after objection period expires
Licensee may enter after objection withdrawn or resolved
Licensee may enter after Authority direction with terms/conditions
Authority may vary, suspend or revoke directions
Failure to comply with direction constitutes offence

sections.source_quote: the licensee must give 14 days' notice to the developer or owner of that land or building, stating as fully and accurately as possible the nature and extent of the acts intended to be done for the purpose of paragraph (a), (b) or (c), as the case may be.

sections.title: Telecom licensees must comply with dispute resolution procedures for infrastructure disputes
sections.summary: Telecommunication licensees must follow a dispute resolution process when landowners object to infrastructure installation. The Authority mediates disputes, and licensees must take genuine steps to resolve conflicts. If unresolved, licensees must report to the Authority with details of actions taken. The Authority may issue binding directions to resolve disputes, including requiring landowners to allow installation or licensees to install systems. Non-compliance with directions is an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs dispute resolution for telecom infrastructure installations across borders, directly impacting data storage locations and physical infrastructure access for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Telecommunication licensee must give 14 days' written notice to landowner/developer
Landowner may lodge written objection within 14 days
Authority must notify licensee to resolve dispute with objector
Licensee must take genuine steps to resolve dispute with objector
If dispute not resolved, licensee must inform Authority and provide details of steps taken
Authority must notify licensee and objector whether licensee took genuine steps to resolve dispute
If Authority satisfied, must hold inquiry on objection with opportunity to be heard
Authority may direct licensee or landowner to give effect to notice with terms/conditions
Licensee may enter on land/building to do acts after objection period expires
Licensee may enter after objection withdrawn or resolved
Licensee may enter after Authority direction with terms/conditions
Authority may vary, suspend or revoke directions
Failure to comply with direction constitutes offence

sections.source_quote: the licensee must give 14 days' notice to the developer or owner of that land or building, stating as fully and accurately as possible the nature and extent of the acts intended to be done

sections.heading: The execution of any work by a public telecommunication
sections.title: Telecom infrastructure must be installed safely without affecting public works
sections.summary: Telecommunication licensees must carry out any work affecting public infrastructure (streets, rivers, waterways, etc.) in a lawful manner that ensures safety of persons or property. This section applies to installation of telecom equipment over, on or under ground. The requirement ensures physical infrastructure installations do not compromise public safety or existing systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates safe installation practices for telecom infrastructure that could impact data storage locations, ensuring physical infrastructure does not compromise data security or safety.

requirements.text: Telecom infrastructure work must be carried out in lawful manner ensuring safety of persons or property

sections.source_quote: The execution of any work by a public telecommunication licensee under this Act which may affect any street, railway, river, canal, or other waterway or any system of irrigation, drainage or water supply or any telecommunications, harbour works or any other public or private works, and the erection of any installation or plant used for telecommunications whether over, on or under the ground must be carried out in a lawful manner having regard to the safety of any person or property.

sections.heading: The Authority may —
Any person who digs, bores, trenches, grades, excavates or
sections.title: Prohibition on unauthorized digging near telecom cables
sections.summary: This section prohibits any person from digging, boring, trenching, grading, excavating, or breaking ground with mechanical equipment or explosives without first ascertaining the location of telecommunication cables belonging to or under the control of a telecommunication system licensee. The offense carries a maximum fine of $100,000 or imprisonment for up to three years. The prohibition applies to all individuals and their employees or agents who perform such activities without proper cable location verification.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on physical activities that could interfere with telecom infrastructure, which may affect data transfer operations if such activities occur near data centers or network facilities. It requires cable location verification before any ground disturbance, relevant for assessing physical security risks in data transfer environments.

requirements.text: Any person who digs, bores, trenches, grades, excavates or breaks ground with mechanical equipment or explosives
Ascertain location of telecommunication cable belonging to or under management/control of telecommunication system licensee
Not perform activities without ascertaining cable location

sections.source_quote: Any person who digs, bores, trenches, grades, excavates or breaks any ground with any mechanical equipment or explosive or allows employee or agent of the person to do so without first ascertaining the location of any telecommunication cable belonging to or under the management or control of any telecommunication system licensee that may be interfered with shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $100,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or to both.

sections.heading: Any person who digs, bores, trenches, grades, excavates or (cont.)
Any person who digs, bores, trenches, grades, excavates or (cont.)
sections.title: Control restrictions on designated trusts and equity interests
sections.summary: This section imposes restrictions on the transfer, disposal, and payment of equity interests in designated trusts. It requires that any transfer, payment, or issuance of equity interests must be expressly permitted by the Authority. The section also outlines specific conditions under which these restrictions apply, including winding up scenarios and the Authority's role in authorizing such actions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by imposing strict controls on equity interests in designated trusts, requiring explicit Authority permission for any transfer or payment of equity interests. This impacts cross-border data transfers involving trust structures.

requirements.text: No equity interests in the designated trust may be issued or offered without explicit Authority permission
No amount may be paid in respect of specified equity interests without explicit Authority authorization

sections.source_quote: no equity interests in the designated trust are to be issued or offered (whether by way of rights, bonus or otherwise) in respect of the specified equity interests, unless the Authority expressly permits such issue or offer; and except in a winding up of the designated trust, no amount is to be paid (whether by way of profits, income or otherwise) in respect of the specified equity interests, unless the Authority expressly authorises such payment.

sections.heading: In this Part —
In this Part — (cont.)
In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for telecommunication licensees and dispute resolution
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Part, including 'declared telecommunication licensee' (a licensee approved by the Authority), 'dispute resolution scheme' (a scheme established or approved by the Authority for resolving subscriber-telecom disputes), and 'subscriber' (an end user of telecommunication services). These definitions establish the scope of applicability for the Part's provisions.

requirements.text: A declared telecommunication licensee must be approved by the Authority
The declaration must be notified in the Gazette
A dispute resolution scheme must be established or approved by the Authority
A subscriber is defined as an end user subscribing to telecommunication services

sections.source_quote: 50. In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — "declared telecommunication licensee" means a telecommunication licensee — (a) that is declared by the Authority to be a telecommunication licensee subject to this Part; or (b) that is within a class of telecommunication licensees declared by the Authority to be a class of telecommunication licensees subject to this Part, and the declaration must be notified in the Gazette; "dispute resolution scheme" means a dispute resolution scheme established or approved by the Authority under section 51(1); "subscriber" means an end user that subscribes to a telecommunication service from a telecommunication licensee.

sections.heading: The Authority may, with the approval of the Minister, make
sections.title: Regulations for dispute resolution scheme operators
sections.summary: The Authority, with Minister approval, may make regulations for dispute resolution scheme operators, covering service standards, fees, record-keeping, reporting requirements, and administrative matters. These regulations ensure proper operation of dispute resolution mechanisms for telecommunication services.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulations for dispute resolution scheme operators may affect cross-border data transfers by establishing standards for resolving international subscriber disputes.

requirements.text: The Authority may make regulations for dispute resolution scheme operators
Regulations must cover service standards for dispute resolution schemes
Regulations must specify fees for dispute resolution services
Regulations must define record-keeping requirements and retention periods
Regulations must specify reporting requirements to the Authority

sections.source_quote: 52. The Authority may, with the approval of the Minister, make regulations under section 97 to provide for matters relating to the operations of an operator of a dispute resolution scheme, including — (a) the standards or requirements of the services provided under the dispute resolution scheme; (b) the fees that the operator may charge for the services provided under the dispute resolution scheme; (c) the records that the operator must keep and the period of retention of such records; (d) the reports that the operator must submit to the Authority, and the manner and time for such submission; (e) matters relating to the administration of the dispute resolution scheme; and (f) generally to give effect to or for carrying out the purposes of this Part.

sections.heading: Subject to such conditions as the Authority may impose,
Any person who knowing or having reason to believe that a
Any person who —
sections.title: Fraudulent retention of telecommunication messages
sections.summary: Fraudulently retaining or wilfully divulging messages or records that ought to have been delivered to another person is an offence. Similarly, neglecting to deliver messages or records when required by a telecommunication licensee is also an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section regulates message handling in telecommunication systems, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving international messaging services.

requirements.text: A person must not fraudulently retain or wilfully divulge messages that ought to be delivered
A person must not neglect to deliver messages when required by a telecommunication licensee

sections.source_quote: 65. Any person who — (a) fraudulently retains or wilfully divulges, makes away with or detains a message or record of a message which ought to have been delivered to some other person; or (b) being required by a public telecommunication licensee to deliver up any such message or record of such message neglects or refuses to do so, shall be guilty of an offence.

sections.heading: Any
sections.title: False representation of telecommunication installations
sections.summary: Placing or maintaining signs that falsely indicate a house or place is part of a telecommunication licensee's installation is an offence. This section prevents misleading representations of telecommunication infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses false representations of telecommunication infrastructure, which may impact cross-border data transfers by ensuring accurate identification of telecommunication facilities.

requirements.text: A person must not place signs that falsely indicate a house is part of a telecommunication licensee's installation

sections.source_quote: 67. Any person who, without the permission of a public telecommunication licensee, places or maintains in or on any house or place, belonging to or under the control of the person, any word, letter or mark which signifies or implies or may reasonably lead the public to believe that the house or place is part of a public telecommunication licensee's installation or plant shall be guilty of an offence.

sections.heading: For
sections.title: International telecommunication service agreements with foreign authorities
sections.summary: Public telecommunication licensees in Singapore may directly communicate with foreign telecommunication authorities or international agencies for facilities, rates, payment terms, operational purposes, or other functions necessary for their operations. This section permits cross-border telecommunication service arrangements subject to the Telecommunications Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows cross-border telecommunication service agreements with foreign authorities but requires compliance with international obligations and government oversight.

requirements.text: Public telecommunication licensee may enter into direct communication with lawfully constituted telecommunication authority of any country
Public telecommunication licensee may enter into direct communication with duly authorised international agency or organisation concerned with telecommunication matters
For purposes of providing facilities, fixing rates, arranging terms of payment or accounting
For operational, engineering or administrative purposes
For any other purpose necessary for proper fulfilment of functions

sections.source_quote: 72. For the purposes of the conduct of any international telecommunication service by a public telecommunication licensee, and subject to this Act, the licensee may enter into direct communication, arrangement and agreement with the lawfully constituted telecommunication authority of any country or with any duly authorised international agency or organisation concerned with telecommunication matters —

sections.heading: A public telecommunication licensee is fully responsible for
sections.title: Financial obligations for international telecommunication services
sections.summary: Public telecommunication licensees must settle all financial obligations arising from international telecommunication services and account with other telecommunication authorities. This section establishes financial accountability for cross-border telecommunication transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires licensees to settle financial obligations with foreign telecommunication authorities for international services.

requirements.text: Public telecommunication licensee is fully responsible for meeting all financial obligations arising from operation of any international telecommunication service
Public telecommunication licensee must settle accounts with other telecommunication authorities

sections.source_quote: 74. A public telecommunication licensee is fully responsible for meeting all financial obligations arising from the operation of any international telecommunication service and must settle accounts with other telecommunication authorities.

sections.heading: Where the Government considers it necessary that any
sections.title: Government contribution for exceptional telecommunication services
sections.summary: Government may contribute to capital outlay for exceptional telecommunication services when a licensee finds it uneconomical to provide such services without government contribution. This section addresses financial support for specialized services.
sections.dtia_summary: Government may provide capital contributions for exceptional telecommunication services when deemed necessary by the government.

requirements.text: Government may make contribution towards capital outlay necessary to provide exceptional telecommunication services
Contribution is estimated by licensee and agreed by government

sections.source_quote: 75. Where the Government considers it necessary that any telecommunication service of an exceptional nature should be provided, and where a public telecommunication licensee considers it uneconomic to provide the service without contribution from the Government, the Government may make such contribution towards the capital outlay necessary to provide any such service as may be estimated by the licensee and agreed to by the Government.

sections.heading: Where the Government considers it necessary that any (cont.)
In any proceedings before any court in respect of any
sections.title: Costs and damages for seized equipment
sections.summary: In court proceedings regarding seized equipment, articles, books, or documents, a person is not entitled to costs or damages unless the seizure was made without reasonable or probable cause.
sections.dtia_summary: Seized equipment must be returned or paid for if seizure was without reasonable cause.

requirements.text: Person not entitled to costs or damages in court proceedings for seized equipment unless seizure made without reasonable or probable cause

sections.source_quote: 83. In any proceedings before any court in respect of any equipment, article, book or document seized in the exercise or the purported exercise of any power conferred under this Act, a person is not entitled to the costs of the proceedings or to any damages or other relief other than an order for the return of the equipment, article, book or document or the payment of their value unless the seizure was made without reasonable or probable cause.

sections.heading: Where an offence under this Act or any regulations made under
sections.title: Director liability for offences committed by company
sections.summary: This section states that directors, managers, secretaries, or partners of a company who commit offences under the Telecommunications Act or its regulations are liable for punishment unless they prove the offence was committed without their consent or connivance and they exercised due diligence in preventing it. The liability applies to individuals acting in such capacities at the time of the offence.

requirements.text: A person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a director, manager, secretary or other similar officer or a partner of the company shall be guilty of the offence
A person who was purporting to act in such capacity shall be guilty of the offence
The person must prove the offence was committed without their consent or connivance
The person must prove they exercised all such diligence to prevent the commission of the offence as they ought to have exercised

sections.source_quote: Where an offence under this Act or any regulations made under this Act has been committed by a company, firm, society or other body of persons, any person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a director, manager, secretary or other similar officer or a partner of the company, firm, society or other body of persons or was purporting to act in such capacity shall be guilty of that offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly unless he or she proves that — (a) the offence was committed without his or her consent or connivance; and (b) he or she exercised all such diligence to prevent the commission of the offence as he or she ought to have exercised, having regard to the nature of his or her functions in that capacity and to all the circumstances.

sections.heading: If any sum required to be paid by a telecommunication licensee
If any sum required to be paid by a telecommunication licensee (cont.)
sections.title: Telecom licensees must transfer business and property to separate entities
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Minister may direct a telecommunication licensee to transfer business operations and property to a separate entity or independent entity. The transfer must be conducted in a manner that ensures the licensee does not gain effective control over the receiving entity. The Minister must ensure the directions are proportionate to eliminate competition-distorting practices. The transfer takes effect immediately without further assurance, and existing agreements and liabilities continue to bind the transferee.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires separation of telecom business operations to prevent competition distortion; relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border transfers of telecom assets.

requirements.text: The Minister may direct a telecommunication licensee to transfer business operations and property to a separate entity or independent entity
The separate entity must apply for a new telecommunication licence
The transfer must ensure the licensee does not gain effective control over the receiving entity
The Minister must ensure directions are proportionate to eliminate competition-distorting practices
The transfer takes effect immediately without further assurance
Existing agreements and liabilities continue to bind the transferee

sections.source_quote: The separation order may — (a) direct the relevant telecommunication licensee to transfer to a separate entity or an independent entity either or both of the following: (i) the whole or any part of any business of the relevant telecommunication licensee that is conducted pursuant to a telecommunication licence granted under section 5; (ii) the whole or any part of any property of the relevant telecommunication licensee that is used by it to provide any telecommunication services, and any rights, obligations and liabilities relating to that property

sections.heading: A public telecommunication licensee is not liable in respect of
sections.title: Telecom licensees not liable for service failures due to third parties
sections.summary: This section states that a public telecommunication licensee is not liable for injuries, losses, or damages caused by failures in service provision, communication interruptions, transmission errors, or loss of secrecy due to the acts or defaults of others or accidents beyond their control. The liability exemption applies to service failures that are not attributable to the licensee's own actions or negligence.
sections.dtia_summary: Exempts telecom licensees from liability for service failures caused by third parties; relevant for DTIA when assessing service continuity and third-party risks.

requirements.text: A public telecommunication licensee is not liable for injuries, losses, or damages caused by failures in service provision
A public telecommunication licensee is not liable for communication interruptions or transmission errors
A public telecommunication licensee is not liable for loss of secrecy due to third parties

sections.source_quote: A public telecommunication licensee is not liable in respect of any injury, loss or damage suffered by any person by reason of — (a) any failure to provide or delay in providing any telecommunication services or any equipment associated therewith or service ancillary thereto; (b) any failure, interruption, suspension or restriction of any telecommunication service or service ancillary thereto or delay of, or fault in, any communication by means of telecommunications; (c) any error in, or omission of, any information transmitted through telecommunications; or (d) any loss of secrecy in communication arising from the use of any telecommunication service, which is due to the act or default of another person, or an accident or some other cause beyond the public telecommunication licensee's control.

sections.heading: Act 12 of 2020 — Building Control (Amendment) Act 2020
